turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show! Today we're diving into the Transformer architecture and the self-attention mechanism that revolutionized AI. Can you walk us through why attention became such a game-changer compared to what came before?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Before Transformers, models like LSTMs read text like a person reading aloud—one word at a time, carrying forward a fading memory. Self-attention let every word look directly at every other word simultaneously, eliminating that sequential bottleneck and unlocking true parallelism.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's a powerful way to frame it. So when a word "looks at" other words, how does it actually decide what's relevant? Can you explain what's happening mechanically with those queries, keys, and values?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Think of it like a library search. The query is your search term—what you're looking for. Keys are book titles on the shelf—what each book offers. The dot product matches your query to relevant titles, and the values are the actual book contents you retrieve and combine.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: I love that library analogy, it really clicks. Before

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Before Transformers, we used LSTMs and GRUs, which read text like a person reading aloud—one word at a time, carrying context forward in a hidden state. This sequential bottleneck meant you couldn't parallelize training, and by the time you reached word 500, the model had often forgotten word 5.
